blocks.statement: I can apply every Unit 6 tool — v = fλ, reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, Doppler, dB, EM spectrum, mirrors, lenses, Wien's law, and tsunami physics — independently on the transfer task.

blocks.whatWeKnow: Every Unit 6 tool: v = fλ, reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, Doppler, dB, EM spectrum, mirrors, lenses, Wien's law, tsunami v = √(g·d), seismic speeds.
blocks.connection: Problem 5 is the load-bearing R4 prompt: given a radar return + Doppler shift, compute distance + velocity, then predict tsunami arrival on a coast. The whole unit in one problem.

blocks.title: This task is on paper, in class
blocks.markdown: The transfer task itself is completed **on paper during class** — keep your work in the room. This page is your **planning and reflection space** around it. Use it to get ready and to capture your thinking; the graded task lives on the paper packet.

## The task — 5 multi-part problems, ~40 minutes, independent

- **Problem 1 — Wave equation across media.** A 1000 Hz sound moves from air (v = 343 m/s) into water (v = 1480 m/s). Compute λ in each medium. Explain what stays the same and what changes (and why).
- **Problem 2 — Reflection.** A flat mirror is 2.0 m from a 1.6-m-tall person. Locate the image and compute its height. State whether real or virtual. Sketch a 2-ray diagram.
- **Problem 3 — Doppler.** A 1000 Hz ambulance siren moves at 25 m/s toward a stationary listener. What frequency is heard? What about the listener BEHIND the ambulance (away)? Use f ' = f · v / (v ∓ v_source).
- **Problem 4 — EM spectrum.** A radio station broadcasts at f = 540 kHz. Compute λ. Which EM band is this? (Use c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.) Is this AM or FM?
- **Problem 5 — ★ Asteroid scenario.** Radar pulse to 2026-XJ: transmitted f_T = 2.4 GHz, round-trip time Δt = 2.4 s, echo received at f_R = 2.40010 GHz (10 kHz higher). (a) Compute distance. (b) Compute approach velocity. (c) If 2026-XJ impacts the ocean 1500 km from a coast (d_ocean = 4 km), how long until the tsunami arrives at the coast?

### How it's scored — 4 dimensions, each 0–4

- **Science** — correct physics: vectors, kinematics, units.
- **Reasoning** — sound method; uncertainty explained, not just stated.
- **Communication** — work shown, units labeled, organized, readable.
- **Transfer-to-Asteroid** — honest public-facing connection to 2026-XJ.

blocks.title: Allowed today
blocks.markdown: The Unit 6 Equation Reference card and a calculator. Every equation on it is on the MCAS Equation Sheet — you do **not** need to memorize them. You DO need to recognize when each one applies. You may ask **clarifying** questions about what the task means — but not physics-content questions.

blocks.prompt: Before you start the paper task, write your plan: match each of the five problems to its Unit 6 idea, decide what order you'll tackle them, and predict where you will struggle. (Problem 5 is the ★ — it integrates radar + Doppler + tsunami.)
blocks.frame: My plan: first I will ___, then ___. I expect to struggle most with ___.

blocks.markdown:
## After the task — final unit self-assessment

Rate yourself on every Unit 6 target below. This is your end-of-unit Marzano record — it feeds your Mastery Growth Chart and tells us both exactly where to focus before Unit 7 builds on this one.

blocks.prompt: End-of-unit reflection: where did you grow the most this unit, and what's the one idea you want locked in before Unit 7 (electricity & magnetism)?
blocks.frame: I grew most on ___. Before Unit 7 I want to lock in ___.
